Eventually, the increasing power of Six Nara Schools and their influence in politics started to overwhelm the city of Nara. This forced [Emperor Kanmu](/wiki/Emperor_Kanmu "Emperor Kanmu") to relocate the capital, moving it to [Heian-kyō](/wiki/Heian-ky%C5%8D "Heian-kyō") (present-day [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto")). It also directly encouraged the creation of the [Tendai](/wiki/Tendai "Tendai") school, founded by [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō"), and the [Shingon](/wiki/Shingon "Shingon") school, founded by [Kūkai](/wiki/K%C5%ABkai "Kūkai").

* **[Tendai Buddhism](/wiki/Tendai_Buddhism "Tendai Buddhism") (天台宗, *Tendai-shū*)** is a branch of the [Tiantai Buddhism](/wiki/Tiantai_Buddhism "Tiantai Buddhism") introduced to Japan by [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō") in 805, who also introduced tantric elements into the tradition. The primary text of Tendai Buddhism is the *[Lotus Sutra](/wiki/Lotus_Sutra "Lotus Sutra")*, but the *[Nirvana Sutra](/wiki/Nirvana_Sutra "Nirvana Sutra")* and esoteric works like the *[Mahavairocana Sutra](/wiki/Mahavairocana_Sutra "Mahavairocana Sutra")* (大日経, *Dainichikyō*) and *[Vajrasekhara Sutra](/wiki/Vajrasekhara_Sutra "Vajrasekhara Sutra")* (金剛頂経, *Kongōchōkyō*) are also important. The Lotus Sutra is also central to Tendai esoteric teachings.
* **[Shingon Buddhism](/wiki/Shingon_Buddhism "Shingon Buddhism") (真言宗, *Shingon-shū*)** is a branch of the [Vajrayana Buddhism](/wiki/Vajrayana_Buddhism "Vajrayana Buddhism") introduced to Japan by [Kūkai](/wiki/K%C5%ABkai "Kūkai") in 816, who traveled to China and studied the [Chinese Mantrayana tradition](/wiki/Chinese_Esoteric_Buddhism "Chinese Esoteric Buddhism"). Shingon is based mainly on two tantric scriptures, the *Mahavairocana Sutra* and the *Vajrasekhara Sutra*. [Dainichi Nyorai](/wiki/Dainichi_Nyorai "Dainichi Nyorai") is central to Shingon esoteric teachings.

Additionally, this period saw the development of **[Shugendō](/wiki/Shugend%C5%8D "Shugendō")**, an eclectic tradition which brought together Buddhist and ancient [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto") elements. It was founded by [En no Gyōja](/wiki/En_no_Gy%C5%8Dja "En no Gyōja") (役行者, *"En the ascetic"*).

### Kamakura Buddhism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhism_in_Japan&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Kamakura Buddhism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Chionin31n3200.jpg/250px-Chionin31n3200.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chionin31n3200.jpg)

[Chion-in](/wiki/Chion-in "Chion-in"), the head temple of [Jōdo-shū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo-sh%C5%AB "Jōdo-shū").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Eiheiji_map.jpg/250px-Eiheiji_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eiheiji_map.jpg)

A traditional map of [Eihei-ji](/wiki/Eihei-ji "Eihei-ji"), the main temple of the [Sōtō](/wiki/S%C5%8Dt%C5%8D "Sōtō") school.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Hiroshige_Temple_compound_on_a_hill.jpg/250px-Hiroshige_Temple_compound_on_a_hill.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hiroshige_Temple_compound_on_a_hill.jpg)

A print of the [Nichiren Shū](/wiki/Nichiren_Sh%C5%AB "Nichiren Shū") temple [Ikegami Honmon-ji](/wiki/Ikegami_Honmon-ji "Ikegami Honmon-ji") by [Hiroshige](/wiki/Hiroshige "Hiroshige").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Daruma-Bodhidharma.jpg/250px-Daruma-Bodhidharma.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daruma-Bodhidharma.jpg)

Bodhidharma ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters"): 達磨; [Hiragana](/wiki/Hiragana "Hiragana"): だるま; [Rōmaji](/wiki/R%C5%8Dmaji "Rōmaji"): *Daruma*), painted by [Miyamoto Musashi](/wiki/Miyamoto_Musashi "Miyamoto Musashi"), swordsman artist and philosopher close to [Takuan Soho](/wiki/Takuan_Soho "Takuan Soho") monk of the Rinzai school (linked to the [samurai caste](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai")) founded by the 28th Patriarch.

During the [Kamakura period](/wiki/Kamakura_period "Kamakura period"), many Buddhist schools (classified by scholars as "New Buddhism" or *Shin Bukkyo*), as opposed to "Old Buddhism" *(Kyū Bukkyō)*.

* **[Pure Land Buddhism](/wiki/Pure_Land_Buddhism#Japanese_Pure_Land "Pure Land Buddhism")**, which focuses on [Nembutsu](/wiki/Nembutsu "Nembutsu") practice, chanting the name of [Amida Buddha](/wiki/Amida_Buddha "Amida Buddha"), so as to be reborn in the [Pure Land](/wiki/Pure_Land "Pure Land").
  + The **[Jōdo-shū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo-sh%C5%AB "Jōdo-shū")** founded by [Hōnen](/wiki/H%C5%8Dnen "Hōnen") (1133–1212).
  + The **[Jōdo Shinshū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo_Shinsh%C5%AB "Jōdo Shinshū")** founded by [Shinran](/wiki/Shinran "Shinran") (1173–1263).
  + The **[Ji-shū](/wiki/Ji-sh%C5%AB "Ji-shū")** founded by [Ippen](/wiki/Ippen "Ippen") (1239–1289).
  + The **[Yūzū-Nembutsu-shū](/wiki/Yuzu_Nembutsu "Yuzu Nembutsu")** founded by [Ryōnin](/wiki/Ry%C5%8Dnin "Ryōnin") (1072–1132).
* **[Zen Buddhism](/wiki/Japanese_Zen "Japanese Zen")**, originating from Chinese [Chan Buddhism](/wiki/Chan_Buddhism "Chan Buddhism"), focuses on [Zazen](/wiki/Zazen "Zazen") meditation.
  + The **[Rinzai Zen](/wiki/Rinzai_Zen "Rinzai Zen")** founded by [Eisai](/wiki/Eisai "Eisai") (1141–1215), a Japanese line of the Chinese [Linji school](/wiki/Linji_school "Linji school"), emphasizes [Kōan](/wiki/K%C5%8Dan "Kōan") practice.
  + The **[Sōtō Zen](/wiki/S%C5%8Dt%C5%8D_Zen "Sōtō Zen")** founded by [Dōgen](/wiki/D%C5%8Dgen "Dōgen") (1200–1253), a Japanese line of the Chinese [Caodong school](/wiki/Caodong_school "Caodong school"), emphasizes [Shikantaza](/wiki/Shikantaza "Shikantaza") practice.
  + The **[Ōbaku Zen](/wiki/%C5%8Cbaku "Ōbaku")** introduced by [Ingen](/wiki/Ingen "Ingen") in 1654, incorporates elements of [Nembutsu](/wiki/Nembutsu "Nembutsu") practice.
* **[Nichiren Buddhism](/wiki/Nichiren_Buddhism "Nichiren Buddhism")**, founded by [Nichiren](/wiki/Nichiren "Nichiren") (1222–1282) which focuses on the Lotus Sutra and [Odaimoku](/wiki/Odaimoku "Odaimoku") practice, chanting the name of the Lotus Sutra.
  + The **[Nichiren-shū](/wiki/Nichiren-sh%C5%AB "Nichiren-shū")**, venerates and reveres the [Shakyamuni Buddha of the Essential Teachings](/wiki/Eternal_Buddha "Eternal Buddha").
  + The **[Nichiren Shōshū](/wiki/Nichiren_Sh%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB "Nichiren Shōshū")**, venerates and reveres the [Dai Gohonzon of the High Sanctuary of the Essential Teachings](/wiki/Dai_Gohonzon "Dai Gohonzon").

### New religious movements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhism_in_Japan&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: New religious movements")]

There are various Japanese new religious movements which can be considered Buddhist sects, the largest of these are lay Nichiren Buddhist groups such as [Soka Gakkai](/wiki/Soka_Gakkai "Soka Gakkai"), [Reiyūkai](/wiki/Reiy%C5%ABkai "Reiyūkai"), [Risshō Kōsei-kai](/wiki/Rissh%C5%8D_K%C5%8Dsei_Kai "Risshō Kōsei Kai") and lay Zen Buddhist groups such as [Sanbo Kyodan](/wiki/Sanbo_Kyodan "Sanbo Kyodan") and FAS Society. But there are other new movements such as [Agon Shū](/wiki/Agon_Shu "Agon Shu") (阿含宗, *"Agama School"*), a Buddhist school which focuses on studying the *[Agamas](/wiki/%C4%80gama_(Buddhism) "Āgama (Buddhism)")*, a collection of [early Buddhist scriptures.](/wiki/Early_Buddhist_texts "Early Buddhist texts")

## Cultural influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhism_in_Japan&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Cultural influence")]

### Societal influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhism_in_Japan&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Societal influence")]

During the Kamakura (1185–1333) and Muromachi (1336–1573) periods, Buddhism and Buddhist institutions had a great influence on Japanese society. Buddhist institutions were used by the shogunate to control the country. During the Edo (1600–1868) this power was constricted, to be followed by persecutions at the beginning of the [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era") (1868–1912).[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnelling1987-87) Buddhist temples played a major administrative role during the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period"), through the [Danka](/wiki/Danka_system "Danka system") or *terauke* system. In this, Japanese citizens were required to register at their local Buddhist temples and obtain a certification (*terauke*), which became necessary to function in society. At first, this system was put into place to suppress [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Japan "Christianity in Japan"), but over time it took on the larger role of census and population control.

### Artistic influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhism_in_Japan&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Artistic influence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/WindGods.JPG/500px-WindGods.JPG)](/wiki/File:WindGods.JPG)

Iconographical evolution of the Wind God.  
 Left: Greek wind god from [Hadda, Afghanistan](/wiki/Hadda,_Afghanistan "Hadda, Afghanistan"), 2nd century.  
 Middle: wind god from [Kizil Caves](/wiki/Kizil_Caves "Kizil Caves"), [Tarim Basin](/wiki/Tarim_Basin "Tarim Basin"), 7th century.  
 Right: Japanese wind god [Fūjin](/wiki/F%C5%ABjin "Fūjin"), 17th century.

Japanese Buddhist art started to develop as soon as the country converted to Buddhism in 548. Some tiles from the [Asuka period](/wiki/Asuka_period "Asuka period") (shown above), the first period following the conversion of the country to Buddhism, display a strikingly classical style, with ample Hellenistic dress and realistically rendered body shape characteristic of [Greco-Buddhist art](/wiki/Greco-Buddhist_art "Greco-Buddhist art").

Buddhist art became extremely varied in its expression. Many elements of Greco-Buddhist art remain to this day however, such as the [Hercules](/wiki/Hercules "Hercules") inspiration behind the [Nio](/wiki/Nio_(Buddhism) "Nio (Buddhism)") guardian deities in front of Japanese Buddhist temples, or representations of the Buddha reminiscent of Greek art such as the Buddha in [Kamakura](/wiki/Kamakura "Kamakura").[[a]](#cite_note-149)

#### Deities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhism_in_Japan&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Deities")]

Main article: [Japanese Buddhist pantheon](/wiki/Japanese_Buddhist_pantheon "Japanese Buddhist pantheon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Heracles-Shukongoshin.JPG/500px-Heracles-Shukongoshin.JPG)](/wiki/File:Heracles-Shukongoshin.JPG)

Iconographical evolution from the Greek god [Heracles](/wiki/Heracles "Heracles") to the Japanese god Shukongōshin. From left to right:  
 1) Heracles (Louvre Museum).  
 2) Heracles on coin of [Greco-Bactrian](/wiki/Greco-Bactrian "Greco-Bactrian") king [Demetrius I](/wiki/Demetrius_I_of_Bactria "Demetrius I of Bactria").  
 3) [Vajrapani](/wiki/Vajrapani "Vajrapani"), the protector of the Buddha, depicted as Heracles in the Greco-Buddhist art of [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara").  
 4) [Shukongōshin](/wiki/Shukong%C5%8Dshin "Shukongōshin"), manifestation of [Vajrapani](/wiki/Vajrapani "Vajrapani"), as protector deity of Buddhist temples in Japan.

Various other Greco-Buddhist artistic influences can be found in the Japanese Buddhist pantheon, the most striking being that of the Japanese wind god [Fūjin](/wiki/F%C5%ABjin "Fūjin"). In consistency with Greek iconography for the wind god [Boreas](/wiki/Boreas "Boreas"), the Japanese wind god holds above his head with his two hands a draping or "wind bag" in the same general attitude.[[b]](#cite_note-150) The abundance of hair has been kept in the Japanese rendering, as well as exaggerated facial features.

Another Buddhist deity, Shukongōshin, one of the wrath-filled protector deities of Buddhist temples in Japan, is also an interesting case of transmission of the image of the famous Greek god Heracles to East Asia along the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road"). Heracles was used in Greco-Buddhist art to represent [Vajrapani](/wiki/Vajrapani "Vajrapani"), the protector of the Buddha, and his representation was then used in China and Japan to depict the protector gods of Buddhist temples.[[c]](#cite_note-151)

#### Artistic motifs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhism_in_Japan&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Artistic motifs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/NaraVines%26Grapes.JPG/250px-NaraVines%26Grapes.JPG)](/wiki/File:NaraVines%26Grapes.JPG)

Vine and grape scrolls from Nara, 7th century.

The artistic inspiration from Greek floral scrolls is found quite literally in the decoration of Japanese roof tiles, one of the only remaining element of wooden architecture throughout centuries. The clearest ones are from the 7th century Nara temple building tiles, some of them exactly depicting vines and grapes. These motifs have evolved towards more symbolic representations, but essentially remain to this day in many Japanese traditional buildings.[[d]](#cite_note-152)

### Architecture and temples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhism_in_Japan&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Architecture and temples")]

Main articles: [List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)](/wiki/List_of_National_Treasures_of_Japan_(temples) "List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)") and [Japanese Buddhist architecture](/wiki/Japanese_Buddhist_architecture "Japanese Buddhist architecture")

[Soga no Umako](/wiki/Soga_no_Umako "Soga no Umako") built Hōkō-ji, the first temple in Japan, between 588 and 596. It was later renamed as [Asuka-dera](/wiki/Asuka-dera "Asuka-dera") for [Asuka](/wiki/Asuka,_Yamato "Asuka, Yamato"), the name of the capital where it was located. Unlike early [Shinto shrines](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine"), early [Buddhist temples](/wiki/Buddhist_temple "Buddhist temple") were highly ornamental and strictly symmetrical. The early [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") (9th–10th century) saw an evolution of style based on the [mikkyō](/wiki/Mikky%C5%8D "Mikkyō") sects Tendai and [Shingon Buddhism](/wiki/Shingon_Buddhism "Shingon Buddhism"). The [Daibutsuyō](/wiki/Daibutsuy%C5%8D "Daibutsuyō") style and the [Zenshūyō](/wiki/Zensh%C5%ABy%C5%8D "Zenshūyō") style emerged in the late 12th or early 13th century.

## Holidays

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhism_in_Japan&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Holidays")]

The following Japanese Buddhist holidays are celebrated by most, if not all, major Buddhist traditions:[[149]](#cite_note-153)

* **Jan. 1st** – [Japanese New Year](/wiki/Japanese_New_Year "Japanese New Year") (*Shōgatsu*).
* **Feb. 15th** – [Nirvana Day](/wiki/Nirvana_Day "Nirvana Day") (*Nehan-e*). The day at the Buddha was said to have died into [Parinirvana](/wiki/Parinirvana "Parinirvana") (his final vanishing).
* **Mar. 20th** – *[Higan](/wiki/Higan "Higan")-e*, the [Spring Equinox](/wiki/Spring_equinox_(northern_hemisphere) "Spring equinox (northern hemisphere)") celebration.
* **Apr. 8th** – [Buddha's Birthday *(Hanamatsuri*)](/wiki/Buddha%27s_Birthday "Buddha's Birthday"), i.e. Kanbutsu-e (潅仏会) or *Busshō-e* (仏生会).
* **July – Aug.** – *[Obon Festival](/wiki/Obon_Festival "Obon Festival"),* a festival to [honor the spirits of one's ancestors](/wiki/Veneration_of_the_dead "Veneration of the dead").
* **Sept. 21st, approximately** – *[Higan](/wiki/Higan "Higan")-e*, the Autumnal Equinox celebration.
* **Dec. 8th** – [Bodhi Day](/wiki/Bodhi_Day "Bodhi Day") (*Shaka-Jōdō-e* or just *Jōdō-e*), this celebrated the [awakening](/wiki/Bodhi "Bodhi") of the Buddha.
* **Dec. 31st** – *Jōya-e* or *Sechibun-e*, the end of the year celebration.

Some holidays are specific to certain schools or traditions. For example, Zen Buddhist traditions celebrate *Daruma-ki* on October 15 to commemorate the life of [Bodhidharma](/wiki/Bodhidharma "Bodhidharma").

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buddhism_in_Japan&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Demographics")]

See also: [Religion in Japan](/wiki/Religion_in_Japan "Religion in Japan")

According to statistics by the Agency for Cultural Affairs in 2023, the [religious corporation](/wiki/Religious_corporation "Religious corporation") under the jurisdiction of the [Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education,_Culture,_Sports,_Science_and_Technology "Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology") in Japan had 129 million believers, of which 46 million were Buddhists. Most of them were believers of new schools of Buddhism which were established in the [Kamakura period](/wiki/Kamakura_period "Kamakura period") (1185－1333).[[6]](#cite_note-shukyo23-6) According to these statistics, the largest sects of Japanese Buddhism are the [Jōdo Buddhists](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo-sh%C5%AB "Jōdo-shū") with 22 million believers, followed by the [Nichiren](/wiki/Nichiren "Nichiren") Buddhists with 10 million believers.[[6]](#cite_note-shukyo23-6)

There are a wide range of estimates; the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") estimated 36.2% of the population in 2010 practiced Buddhism.[[150]](#cite_note-pewforum.org-154) The Japanese General Social Survey placed the figure at less than 20% of the population in 2017. The 2013 Japanese National Character Survey showed that roughly 70% of the population do not adhere to any religious beliefs.[[2]](#cite_note-Iwai_2017-2) Another survey indicates that about 60% of Japanese families have a [butsudan](/wiki/Butsudan "Butsudan") (Buddhist shrine) in their homes.[[151]](#cite_note-155) According to a 2012 Pew Research study, Japan has the [third largest Buddhist population](/wiki/Buddhism_by_country#By_country "Buddhism by country") in the world, after [China](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism") and [Thailand](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Thailand "Buddhism in Thailand").[[152]](#cite_note-PewDec2012-156)
